science-space.title: Did NASA just find evidence of ancient life on Mars? Perseverance rover spots complex carbon in Red Planet rocks
science-space.summary: NASA's Perseverance rover, a car-sized robotic explorer that has been driving across Mars since 2021, has discovered something potentially groundbreaking: organic molecules (complex carbon-based compounds) in Martian rocks. Organic molecules are the building blocks of life as we know it, though finding them doesn't automatically mean life existed there. What makes this discovery significant is the sheer number of detections—hundreds of them in mudstones from Jezero Crater, making it the strongest evidence yet found on Mars. Scientists are excited because this suggests Mars once had conditions suitable for life billions of years ago. This discovery matters because it could answer one of humanity's biggest questions: Are we alone in the universe?

science-space.title: This is the largest and most detailed image of our Milky Way — with over 60 million stars and 50 exoplanet systems
science-space.summary: The European Space Agency (ESA) released an unprecedented image of the heart of our Milky Way galaxy using their Euclid spacecraft, a telescope designed to map the universe. The image contains over 60 million stars and reveals 50 exoplanet systems (planets orbiting other stars). To put this in perspective, previous images showed far fewer stars and less detail, making this the most comprehensive look at our galaxy's core ever created. This matters because it helps scientists understand how galaxies form, evolve, and how common planets are throughout the universe. It's essentially giving humanity a better map of our cosmic neighborhood and showing us we're not alone—planets are everywhere.

science-space.title: NASA's TESS Mission Reveals the 'Puffiest' Planets Ever Found
science-space.summary: NASA's TESS mission (Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite), a space telescope launched in 2018 to find planets around other stars, has discovered two extraordinary planets nicknamed 'super-puffs.' These planets are as massive as Jupiter (the largest planet in our solar system) but incredibly light—so light that their density is similar to cotton candy. Imagine a planet the size of a beach ball but weighing almost nothing; that's how puffy these worlds are. Scientists are puzzled about how such low-density planets could form, since it challenges current theories about planet formation. These discoveries help astronomers understand the incredible diversity of planets out there and push them to revise their understanding of how planetary systems develop.

science-space.title: NASA to Share Latest Moon Base Mission Progress
science-space.summary: NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman (the head of the entire space agency) will announce updates on NASA's ambitious plan to build a permanent base on the Moon's surface. This Moon Base is a crucial stepping stone for NASA's strategy to return astronauts to the lunar surface and eventually send humans to Mars. Isaacman and Carlos García-Galán, the program manager, will discuss new contracts for lunar lander missions—vehicles that will carry equipment and people down to the Moon. This matters because establishing a Moon base would give humanity a permanent outpost in space, similar to the International Space Station but on the Moon. It represents the next era of human space exploration and brings us closer to becoming a multi-planetary species.

science-space.title: NASA at the Ion: Orion Lessons from Artemis II Shape NASA's Moon to Mars Path
science-space.summary: Branelle Rodriguez, the Orion Spacecraft Vehicle Manager at NASA, reflected on the Artemis II mission, which successfully sent four astronauts around the Moon and back to Earth—marking humanity's first crewed lunar journey in over 50 years. The Orion spacecraft is NASA's next-generation crew vehicle designed to carry astronauts deeper into space than ever before. Rodriguez discussed how lessons learned from Artemis II are shaping NASA's future plans to land people on the Moon again and eventually send them to Mars. The Artemis program represents NASA's long-term vision for deep space exploration and human settlement beyond Earth. Understanding what worked and what needs improvement from Artemis II is essential for making future missions safer and more successful.

science-space.title: NASA's HiRISE Captures Perseverance Marking a Milestone on Mars
science-space.summary: NASA's Perseverance rover reached a remarkable milestone on Mars: it has traveled a full marathon distance (26.2 miles or 42.195 kilometers) across the Red Planet's surface. This achievement occurred after five years and four months of continuous operation, all while conducting scientific experiments. The HiRISE camera (a high-resolution imaging instrument on NASA's Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter satellite) captured a photo of the tiny rover from orbit, showing just how vast and challenging the Martian terrain is. Perseverance's longevity and distance traveled demonstrate the durability of NASA's engineering and the rover's success in exploring Mars. Each mile traveled brings new scientific data that helps us understand whether Mars could have supported life and how to prepare for eventual human missions to the Red Planet.

science-space.title: Roman Telescope Comes to Kennedy
science-space.summary: NASA's Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope, one of the most advanced space telescopes ever built, has arrived at Kennedy Space Center in Florida aboard a specialized barge. The Roman telescope is named after Nancy Grace Roman, a pioneering NASA astronomer who broke barriers as a female scientist in the 1950s-60s. This telescope will study the universe's deepest mysteries, including dark matter, dark energy, and the search for habitable exoplanets. After arriving at Kennedy Space Center, Roman will undergo final preparations and testing before its launch targeted for August 30, 2026. The Roman telescope represents humanity's next major tool for understanding the cosmos and will work alongside other telescopes like the James Webb Space Telescope to expand our knowledge of the universe.

science-space.title: Euclid View of Milky Way Heart Previews Core Survey by NASA's Roman
science-space.summary: The European Space Agency's Euclid spacecraft and NASA's Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope are joining forces to study the heart of our Milky Way galaxy. Euclid has already captured detailed images of this region, and its data is helping scientists prepare for Roman's observations later this year. Think of it like reconnaissance—Euclid is providing a preview that helps Roman's team plan exactly where to look and what to expect. Both telescopes will observe the same area, but each brings different capabilities and tools, making their combined observations far more powerful. This collaboration between NASA and ESA demonstrates how international cooperation in space science leads to better discoveries. By studying the galactic core together, these telescopes will unlock secrets about how galaxies form and evolve.

science-space.title: NASA's aging infrastructure can't handle Artemis launches without $1 billion in upgrades, watchdog warns
science-space.summary: A government watchdog agency (an independent organization that monitors how government agencies spend money) has warned that NASA faces a significant problem: the facilities at Kennedy Space Center and other launch sites are too old and outdated to safely support the Artemis program's ambitious launch schedule. NASA would need approximately $1 billion in upgrades to modernize launch pads, support equipment, and infrastructure. This is important because without these upgrades, NASA cannot reliably launch the rockets needed for Moon missions or support the growing commercial space industry. The warning highlights a real-world challenge to NASA's goals: even great ideas and advanced technology aren't enough without the physical infrastructure to support them. This issue affects not just NASA but also private companies like SpaceX that depend on these facilities.

science-space.title: NASA looks for the origins of interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS
science-space.summary: NASA's James Webb Space Telescope, the most powerful space observatory ever built, is investigating the origins of an unusual visitor to our solar system: comet 3I/ATLAS. The '3I' designation means this is an interstellar comet, one that originated from another star system and traveled through space to reach our neighborhood. This is extremely rare—most comets come from our own solar system. By studying this comet with the James Webb Space Telescope, scientists hope to determine where it came from, how long it's been traveling through space, and what its composition reveals about other star systems. Interstellar objects are cosmic messengers that carry information about the broader universe. Understanding where 3I/ATLAS originated helps us learn about planetary formation processes happening around distant stars.

world-news.title: Iran war live: US vows to defend Gulf interests; Israel kills 2 in Lebanon
world-news.summary: The United States, led by Secretary of State Marco Rubio, is intensifying its military commitment to the Middle East amid escalating tensions with Iran. Israel has killed two people in Lebanon and says it won't withdraw from southern Lebanon, signaling a broader regional conflict. The Gulf region—the area around the Persian Gulf containing major oil reserves and critical shipping routes—is strategically vital to global economics and energy security. This situation matters because conflict in this area could disrupt world oil supplies, affecting gas prices everywhere and potentially drawing NATO (the Western military alliance) into a larger war. These actions represent a significant shift in U.S. foreign policy under the Trump administration toward more aggressive Middle East involvement.

world-news.title: Trump slams NATO over lax participation in Iran war in talk with Mark Rutte
world-news.summary: Donald Trump criticized NATO—the military alliance of 32 countries including the U.S. and European nations—for not doing enough to support American operations against Iran. Mark Rutte, NATO's new secretary general (the top diplomat coordinating the alliance), defended NATO by pointing out that European countries allow the U.S. military to use their bases for operations. This disagreement reveals tension between America and its traditional allies over who should bear the cost of Middle East conflicts. NATO bases in Europe are crucial for U.S. military logistics, meaning European countries are already contributing significantly. Trump's criticism signals he may demand more direct military involvement from European allies, which could reshape decades-old alliances and shift how the West conducts foreign policy.

world-news.title: UN nuclear chief says inspectors will visit Iran sites as part of war deal
world-news.summary: Rafael Grossi, who leads the IAEA (International Atomic Energy Agency—the UN organization that monitors nuclear weapons worldwide), announced that inspectors will be allowed to visit Iranian nuclear facilities as part of potential peace negotiations. However, an Iranian government official said this inspection access would only happen as part of a final agreement with the United States. This matters because Iran's nuclear program has been a major flashpoint in global politics for decades; many Western countries fear Iran could build nuclear weapons, while Iran insists its program is peaceful. Inspections are crucial for verifying whether a country is secretly developing nuclear bombs. These negotiations could either reduce Middle East tensions or collapse, potentially leading to military conflict, which would have devastating global consequences.

world-news.title: France, UK and Spain see record temperatures as heatwave grips western Europe
world-news.summary: Western Europe is experiencing a historic heatwave with temperatures breaking records in France, the UK, and Spain. Tens of millions of people are enduring dangerous heat levels, prompting governments to issue red heat alerts—the highest warning level that tells people to stay inside and avoid outdoor activities. A heatwave is a prolonged period of abnormally hot weather that can cause heat exhaustion, dehydration, and death, especially among elderly people and those without air conditioning. This extreme weather is linked to climate change, the long-term warming of Earth caused by human activities like burning fossil fuels. The crisis matters globally because it shows how climate change is creating immediate, life-threatening dangers in developed countries, forcing governments to reconsider infrastructure and emergency planning to protect their populations.

world-news.title: Biggest city in Crimea without power after Ukraine strikes
world-news.summary: Sevastopol, the largest city in Crimea, lost electrical power after Ukrainian military strikes on infrastructure. Crimea is a peninsula that was part of Ukraine until Russia annexed (forcibly took over) it in 2014, sparking years of conflict. The Russian-appointed governor warned residents that some areas wouldn't have electricity until evening, affecting hundreds of thousands of people. Ukraine has been systematically targeting Russian military and energy infrastructure in Crimea as part of its defense against Russia's invasion that began in 2022. These strikes matter because they demonstrate how the Ukraine-Russia war is intensifying, with Ukraine taking the offensive against Russian-held territory. Power outages can cripple hospitals, water supplies, and communications, showing how modern warfare affects civilian populations and could influence the war's outcome.

world-news.title: Russian opposition leader jailed over anti-war social media posts
world-news.summary: A Russian opposition leader was imprisoned for posting anti-war messages on social media criticizing Russia's invasion of Ukraine. Under President Vladimir Putin's authoritarian government (where power is concentrated in one person with little democratic oversight), speaking against official policies is extremely dangerous and often results in arrest and imprisonment. This represents intensifying political repression in Russia, where the government is cracking down on any dissent or criticism of the war. The incident reveals how authoritarian regimes use fear and imprisonment to silence opposition voices. This matters globally because it shows how the Ukraine war is creating a climate of fear even within Russia, affecting fundamental human rights like freedom of speech. It also indicates the Kremlin's (Russian government's) concern that domestic opposition to the war is growing.

world-news.title: Venezuela struck by back-to-back earthquakes, high casualties feared
world-news.summary: Venezuela experienced two powerful earthquakes in rapid succession—one measuring 7.1 magnitude and another 7.5 magnitude—in the same region near the city of Morón. The U.S. Geological Survey indicated that high casualties are probable, meaning many deaths and injuries are expected. Magnitude measures an earthquake's energy release; anything above 7.0 is considered major and can cause severe destruction to buildings and infrastructure. Venezuela is already suffering from economic collapse, political instability under President Nicolás Maduro, and humanitarian crisis with shortages of food and medicine. This natural disaster compounds these problems by destroying infrastructure, killing people, and stretching already-depleted emergency services. The crisis highlights how developing nations with weak governments and economies are especially vulnerable to natural disasters and have difficulty recovering.

world-news.title: France confirms first Ebola case
world-news.summary: France reported its first confirmed case of Ebola, a rare but extremely dangerous infectious disease, in a French doctor who had been working in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC). Ebola spreads through direct contact with blood or body fluids of infected people and causes severe illness with a high death rate; currently, more than 260 people have died in the DRC outbreak. The disease has no cure, only supportive care to help patients' bodies fight infection. This case matters globally because it shows how diseases can rapidly spread across continents in our interconnected world; the doctor traveled from Africa to France, potentially exposing others. It highlights the danger of Ebola outbreaks in developing countries and the risk of international spread, requiring coordinated global health responses and raising concerns about pandemic preparedness.

world-news.title: Two years on, Kenyan families still seek justice after Gen Z protests
world-news.summary: Two years after Gen Z-led protests in Kenya (demonstrations primarily organized by young people through social media), families of those killed and missing during the unrest say official investigations have stalled and accountability remains elusive. The 2024 protests erupted when Kenyan youth, angered by government corruption and economic hardship, demanded systemic change and reform. Security forces killed dozens of protesters in response, but justice for victims has been slow. This situation matters because it reveals how young people worldwide are increasingly using digital activism to demand accountability from their governments, yet face risks when authorities respond with violence. Kenya's failure to prosecute those responsible for killings sends a message that governments can suppress dissent without consequences, undermining democracy and rule of law across Africa and internationally.

world-news.title: Colombia's left-wing presidential candidate concedes defeat
world-news.summary: Senator Iván Cepeda, a left-wing candidate in Colombia's presidential election, conceded defeat to right-wing businessman Abelardo de la Espriella after losing by less than one percentage point in an extremely close race. This election result signals a potential rightward political shift in Colombia, one of South America's largest economies and a key U.S. ally in the region. Left-wing candidates typically support stronger social programs and wealth redistribution, while right-wing candidates favor business-friendly policies and less government intervention. Colombia's election matters geopolitically because it affects U.S. influence in Latin America, drug trafficking policies, and regional relationships. The razor-thin margin suggests Colombia is deeply divided over its future direction, and the new right-wing government may shift foreign policy and economic strategies, potentially affecting trade relationships and regional stability.

finance-markets.title: Micron posts 15-fold profit surge as AI companies clamour for chips
finance-markets.summary: Micron Technology, one of the world's largest makers of computer memory chips, just reported that its profits jumped 15 times higher than before. This massive jump happened because AI companies like OpenAI and Google are desperately buying memory chips to power their artificial intelligence systems. Think of memory chips like the brain's RAM—AI models need tons of them to work. Micron's stock price jumped in after-hours trading (when regular people can't buy, but big investors can) because investors believe this crazy demand will keep going. This matters to you because it shows how much the AI boom is reshaping which companies make money and grow fastest.

finance-markets.title: Qualcomm reveals Meta as first Big Tech customer for data centre chips
finance-markets.summary: Qualcomm, a major semiconductor company, just announced that Meta (Facebook's parent company) will be their first major tech customer buying specialized computer chips for data centres. Data centres are giant buildings filled with thousands of computers that store and process information on the internet. Qualcomm's stock jumped up to 15 percent on this news because it proves their new chips are good enough for the world's biggest tech companies to use. This is huge because it opens up a completely new market for Qualcomm beyond just making phone chips. For you, this means the infrastructure powering your social media and AI tools is getting more advanced, and different companies are competing fiercely to build it.

finance-markets.title: SK Hynix Surges 12% as US Listing Plan Boosts Valuation Hopes
finance-markets.summary: SK Hynix, a South Korean memory chip company, announced plans to list on the US stock market with a $29 billion valuation (meaning investors think the company is worth $29 billion). A listing means selling shares of the company to the public so anyone with a brokerage account can own a piece of it. When SK Hynix announced this, their stock jumped 12 percent because investors got excited about owning shares in a company that makes the chips every tech device needs. This US listing will also help the company expand its factories and attract international investors. This matters because South Korea's tech industry is becoming even more integrated with American finance and markets, showing how global these technology companies really are.

finance-markets.title: Anthropic accuses Alibaba of obtaining 'illicit' access to Claude
finance-markets.summary: Anthropic, a company that created Claude (a powerful AI chatbot similar to ChatGPT), is accusing Alibaba—China's biggest e-commerce company—of secretly stealing their AI technology. They say Alibaba created thousands of fake accounts to trick Claude into revealing how it works, a technique called 'extracting' capabilities. This is basically industrial espionage: trying to steal someone else's valuable technology without permission. Anthropic is upset because they invested billions developing Claude, and now a Chinese competitor might copy their work. This matters because it shows how intense the AI competition is between America and China, and raises questions about whether governments need new rules to protect AI companies' technology from being stolen, similar to how they protect other inventions.

finance-markets.title: Robot nation: China's bid to beat its demographic decline
finance-markets.summary: China has a huge problem: its workforce is shrinking fast because people aren't having as many babies, and the population is getting older. By the end of this century, China's working-age population could drop to 300 million from over 900 million today. Beijing (China's government) is now betting big on humanoid robots—robots shaped like humans that can do jobs humans used to do—to fill this labour gap and keep the economy running. If robots can manufacture goods, care for elderly people, and do construction, China won't need as many human workers. This matters because China is the world's factory, so if China's workforce collapses, it affects the price of everything you buy globally. It also shows how robotics and automation are becoming solutions to real economic problems, not just sci-fi fantasy.

finance-markets.title: China Starts Marketing Record €5 Billion of Sovereign Bonds
finance-markets.summary: China is selling €5 billion (about $5.7 billion) of government bonds to investors around the world, and this could be the largest amount of euro-denominated bonds China has ever issued. Bonds are basically loans: when you buy a Chinese government bond, you're lending money to China, and they promise to pay you back with interest after a set time period. The euro is the currency used by 20 European countries that are part of the European Union. China issuing record amounts in euros shows they want to reduce their reliance on US dollars and build stronger financial ties with Europe. This matters because it's geopolitical chess: countries use currency choices as a way to build alliances and reduce dependence on rivals, and it affects global money flows and currency values that impact everything from your phone prices to your future vacation costs.

finance-markets.title: US banks would lose $700bn in economic crash, Fed stress tests find
finance-markets.summary: The Federal Reserve (America's central bank) ran stress tests on major Wall Street banks—basically asking 'what would happen to your finances if the economy totally collapsed?'—and found they could lose up to $700 billion combined. The good news is all the banks passed the test, meaning they have enough money saved up to survive even a catastrophic crash. Even better news for banks: because they passed, regulators let them increase payouts to shareholders (investors who own pieces of the bank). The Fed does these tests every year to make sure banks won't collapse like they did in 2008, which nearly destroyed the global economy. This matters because if banks fail, regular people lose their savings, businesses can't get loans, and unemployment skyrockets. The tests show regulators are watching over banks, but also that banks believe they're strong enough to weather almost anything.

finance-markets.title: Thailand Emerges as Southeast Asia's Surprise AI Stock Winner
finance-markets.summary: Thailand's stock market is having its best year in Southeast Asia right now, and the reason might surprise you: investors are buying Thai companies because they believe those companies will benefit from the global AI boom, even though Thailand itself isn't a major AI developer. This is like buying a small company that sells shovels to gold miners during a gold rush—you profit from the trend without being the one finding gold. Thailand's stock exchange is up way more than neighbouring countries like Vietnam, Indonesia, and the Philippines, which shows how investors are hunting for any way to get exposure to AI profits. This matters because it shows how money flows around the world looking for opportunities, how AI hype is reaching even smaller markets, and how stock prices can skyrocket if investors just believe a company will benefit from a big trend, whether that's realistic or not.

finance-markets.title: Brent Oil Erases Wartime Gains as Hormuz Reopening Boosts Supply
finance-markets.summary: Brent oil (a major type of crude oil used globally) has dropped back to where it was before a regional conflict started, because shipping through the Strait of Hormuz—a chokepoint controlling one-third of the world's oil supply—is flowing normally again. During the conflict, people worried ships couldn't get through, so oil prices spiked; now those worries are easing because peace talks between the US and Iran are progressing. The Strait of Hormuz is in the Persian Gulf and is so important that if it closes, global oil prices explode upward, affecting gas prices you pay at the pump and heating costs for homes. Oil prices falling is good for consumers and manufacturers but bad for oil companies' profits. This matters because oil is how ships, planes, and cars move around the world, so any supply disruption ripples through the entire global economy instantly.

finance-markets.title: Japan's Household Assets Rise to ¥2,386 Trillion at End of March
finance-markets.summary: Japanese households (regular families) are getting richer: their total assets jumped 7.1 percent to ¥2,386 trillion (about $14.7 trillion USD) by March 2026, the second-highest on record. Assets include money in bank accounts, stocks you own, real estate, and anything else of value. Japan's household wealth is surging because the stock market has been climbing and real estate values are rising in major cities. This wealth increase matters because when people feel richer, they spend more money, which helps businesses grow and hire more workers. However, it also shows inequality: these assets are concentrated among older, wealthier Japanese people, while younger people struggle to afford homes. For global finance, it matters because wealthy Japan is a major investor in stocks, bonds, and businesses worldwide, so when Japanese households get richer, they invest more internationally and influence global markets.

tech-ai.title: AI was supposed to kill engineering jobs, but new data suggests they're the most resilient
tech-ai.summary: For years, people worried that artificial intelligence would replace engineers and programmers, leading to massive job losses in the tech industry. However, new data from SignalFire (a company that tracks tech industry trends) shows something surprising: engineers are actually being hired more than ever, not less. While AI has caused layoffs in other industries, tech companies are still actively recruiting skilled engineers to build and maintain AI systems. This matters because it challenges the doom-and-gloom narrative about AI destroying jobs—at least in the tech sector. It suggests that as technology evolves, the demand for skilled workers who understand how to work with that technology actually grows rather than shrinks.

tech-ai.title: AI researchers continue to leave Google for its rivals
tech-ai.summary: Top artificial intelligence researchers are leaving Google—one of the world's largest tech companies—to work for competitors like Anthropic, a newer AI company founded by former Google employees. Recently, two prominent researchers, Jonas Adler and Alexander Pritzel, made this move, following other major scientists like Noam Shazeer and John Jumper who left earlier. This matters because these researchers are the brilliant minds behind AI breakthroughs, so losing them could slow Google's AI development. It also shows that talented scientists believe they can do more innovative work elsewhere, suggesting Google might not be the best place for cutting-edge AI research anymore. This brain drain could reshape which companies lead the AI revolution.

tech-ai.title: Europe is pushing back on Washington's chip war
tech-ai.summary: The United States is trying to restrict what advanced computer chips China can buy, hoping to slow China's technological development. Europe, particularly companies like ASML (a Dutch company that makes the machines used to manufacture chips), is pushing back against these restrictions. The MATCH Act is a proposed U.S. law that would ban selling certain chip-making equipment to China. ASML's CEO says this move is unfair because China can only buy older technology anyway—machines from about a decade ago. This matters because it's a geopolitical conflict: the U.S. wants tech dominance over China, but Europe worries these restrictions will hurt European companies' profits and market share. It shows how technology is becoming a weapon in global power struggles.

tech-ai.title: The memory chip crunch is paying off for this US company
tech-ai.summary: A U.S. company made massive profits from a shortage of memory chips—the components that store data in computers and phones. Their revenue quadrupled to $41.45 billion, and even more impressively, their profit jumped from $1.88 billion to $28.2 billion in one year. Memory chips are essential for everything from smartphones to data centers (the computers that power cloud services like Google Drive), so shortages drive up prices and profits for companies that can supply them. This matters because it shows how supply and demand works in tech: when something is scarce, companies that have it can charge premium prices. However, it also highlights a problem—if one company is making extraordinary profits from scarcity, it suggests the chip supply chain is still imbalanced, which could affect consumers and other tech companies.

tech-ai.title: A new paper argues Microsoft exaggerated its quantum claims a year ago
tech-ai.summary: Microsoft announced a major breakthrough in quantum computing last year, claiming to have created a revolutionary processor called Majorana 1 with new topological qubits (a type of quantum computing building block). However, a new scientific paper published in Nature magazine questions whether Microsoft's claims were accurate. Quantum computers are theoretically much faster than regular computers for solving certain problems, so breakthroughs matter globally. A topological qubit is supposed to be more stable and reliable than existing quantum technology. This matters because if Microsoft exaggerated its achievements, it means the quantum computing revolution might be further away than investors and the public thought. It also shows the importance of peer review (when other scientists check each other's work) in technology—bold claims need solid evidence.

tech-ai.title: New website names and shames companies that still don't offer passkeys to users
tech-ai.summary: A new website is calling out major companies that don't offer passkeys, a security feature that's way better than traditional passwords. Passkeys use fingerprints, face recognition, or security keys instead of memorized passwords—they're considered the most secure way to log into apps and services. According to this website, 24% of the world's most popular websites still don't support passkeys, meaning hundreds of millions of people are stuck using weaker security methods. This matters because passwords are constantly hacked; hackers can steal them through data breaches or tricky phishing emails, but passkeys are nearly impossible to steal. The website is shaming companies into upgrading because the pressure from users and the internet community is often what forces large corporations to improve security. If you use the internet, this affects you directly—better security at major websites means your personal information is safer.

tech-ai.title: Companies are scrambling to stop employees from maxing out AI budgets with small tasks
tech-ai.summary: Companies are now rationing how much their employees can spend on AI tools, a dramatic shift from just months ago when AI budgets seemed unlimited. 'Tokenmaxxing' refers to employees using AI chatbots (like ChatGPT) for small, inexpensive tasks to maximize their AI spending, essentially wasting their company's budget on minor work. Companies realized this was costing them way more money than expected, so they're implementing 'token rationing'—basically limiting how much AI each employee can use per day or month. This matters because it shows how quickly tech bubbles can deflate: companies went from spending wildly on AI to being cautious almost overnight. It also reveals that AI, while powerful, might be less economical for routine tasks than companies initially thought. If you're thinking about AI's future, this shows the hype cycle: extreme optimism gives way to realistic cost-benefit analysis.

tech-ai.title: Former Infosys chief has a new startup that wants to challenge the IT services world
tech-ai.summary: Vishal Sikka, who previously led Infosys (a major Indian IT services company), has started a new venture backed by investors like Mayfield and Aramco Ventures (Saudi Arabia's venture capital fund). His startup is recruiting experienced people from major tech companies like SAP and Infosys, plus AI specialists from VianAI. IT services companies help other businesses manage their technology and software systems—it's a huge, lucrative industry. Sikka's team wants to challenge the established players by offering something different or better. This matters because it shows how successful tech executives are betting on new ideas outside established companies, suggesting there's room for disruption even in traditional tech services. It also highlights how AI expertise is becoming so valuable that new startups can attract top talent by promising to work on cutting-edge AI problems rather than routine IT work.

tech-ai.title: Google is finally opening the Play Store to outside payments
tech-ai.summary: Google is changing how it makes money from the Play Store, the app marketplace where Android users (that's most of the world's smartphone users) download apps. Currently, Google takes a flat 30% cut from every app purchase and subscription—meaning if you buy a $10 app, Google keeps $3. A court ruled this was unfair monopoly behavior (a monopoly is when one company controls a market), following a lawsuit by Epic Games. Google now says it will replace this flat 30% fee with 'lower, decoupled fees,' which means different charges for different services. This matters because it could make apps cheaper for you, and it gives app developers more options for how they sell their products. It's also a huge deal for tech regulation—it shows that courts can force even massive companies to change their business practices if they're being unfair to competition.

tech-ai.title: Cerebras stock plunges after earnings as CEO says margin outlook was misunderstood
tech-ai.summary: Cerebras, an AI chip company that recently went public (sold stock on the market for the first time), shocked investors by announcing narrower profit margins than expected in its core business. A margin is the difference between what a company charges for something and what it costs to make—the bigger the margin, the more profit per sale. The company's stock price dropped significantly because investors thought the company would be more profitable than it actually is. This matters because Cerebras is supposed to be a promising AI chip company competing against giants like Nvidia, so disappointing margins suggest either their chips aren't as valuable as hoped or their manufacturing costs are higher than expected. When stock prices drop like this, it affects the entire AI chip industry's credibility and can slow down funding for other AI startups. It's a reminder that even companies with exciting technology need solid business economics to succeed.

energy-climate.title: Mombasa: Key outcomes from the Our Ocean Conference in Kenya
energy-climate.summary:
A major ocean conference has ended in Mombasa, Kenya, with just a handful of countries...
The post Mombasa: Key outcomes from the Our Ocean Conference in Kenya appeared first on Carbon Brief.

energy-climate.title: Turkish PV keeps growing amid policy shift, financing constraints
energy-climate.summary:
In an interview with pv magazine, the Chairman of the Board of Directors of Turkish PV association Günder, Mehmet İzzet Özaydın, explained that Türkiye’s solar market is being driven by strong industrial demand and a competitiveness-focused investment culture.
The post Turkish PV keeps growing amid policy shift, financing constraints appeared first on pv magazine Global.

energy-climate.title: Czech PV market faces policy reset, supply-chain uncertainty
energy-climate.summary:
Jan Krcmar, Director of the Czech Solar Association, tells pv magazine how continued C&I-driven growth and regulatory reforms targeting building codes, storage tariffs, and permitting are reshaping Czechia's long-term energy transition.
The post Czech PV market faces policy reset, supply-chain uncertainty appeared first on pv magazine Global.

energy-climate.title: Grid-forming BESS to enhance stability in utility-scale PV
energy-climate.summary:
Scientists have simulated a 100 MW solar PV plant and a 35 MW / 60 MWh BESS with grid-forming control. In all cases, the grid-forming control provided immediate active/reactive support. Research: we provide quantitative evidence that grid-forming control strategies can support the transition to inverter-dominated power systems.
The post Grid-forming BESS to enhance stability in utility-scale PV appeared first on pv magazine Global.

energy-climate.title: Paving the way for lithium–nitrogen batteries, one hurdle at a time
energy-climate.summary:
Researchers from Belgium and China have proposed a roadmap to advance lithium–nitrogen (Li–N₂) batteries, identifying key barriers such as poor reversibility, slow nitrogen activation, and unstable cell components. They suggest improved electrolytes, catalysts, separators, and cell designs, alongside standardized testing and flow-type architectures, to enable both energy storage and nitrogen-based chemical production.
The post Paving the way for lithium–nitrogen batteries, one hurdle at a time a

energy-climate.title: Microquanta launches 26%-efficient colored perovskite-silicon tandem modules for building-integrated PV
energy-climate.summary:
Microquanta has launched customizable colored perovskite-silicon tandem BIPV modules that combine architectural finishes such as marble effects with the company's four-terminal tandem technology, aiming to reduce the performance penalty typically associated with colored solar façades.
The post Microquanta launches 26%-efficient colored perovskite-silicon tandem modules for building-integrated PV appeared first on pv magazine Global.

energy-climate.title: Sweden’s solar market stabilizes as storage pushes deployment
energy-climate.summary:
In a chat with pv magazine, Ludvig Bydén, Head of power market & permitting at Svensk Solenergi, said Sweden’s solar market is stabilizing after a two-year downturn. He also highlighted a rapid shift toward storage-first investments.
The post Sweden’s solar market stabilizes as storage pushes deployment appeared first on pv magazine Global.

energy-climate.title: HiTHIUM launches ARKVOLT residential energy storage products in Europe
energy-climate.summary:
Building on its GWh-scale expertise in utility-scale energy storage, HiTHIUM has brought its industrial-grade safety standards and system reliability into residential applications with the new ARKVOLT product series, which made its official debut at The smarter E Europe in Munich this week. ARKVOLT is HiTHIUM's premium residential energy storage brand, developed specifically for mature markets including Europe, North America and Australia.
The post HiTHIUM launches ARKVOLT residential energy sto

energy-climate.title: Energy sharing, storage reshaping Slovenia’s PV market
energy-climate.summary:
pv magazine spoke with Nina Hojnik, General Manager of the Slovenian Photovoltaic Association at the Smarter E tradeshow in Munich, about the launch of energy sharing and its first registrations, alongside a residential rebound driven by solar-plus-storage.
The post Energy sharing, storage reshaping Slovenia’s PV market appeared first on pv magazine Global.

energy-climate.title: Escaping the energy straitjacket
energy-climate.summary:
Europe’s split grid infrastructure and lack of central planning mean it fails to use its renewables. It is stuck in volatile prices driven by reliance on imported fossil fuels. Treating the system as one network rather than separate national grids would unlock PV, storage and wind, says Christian Kjaer, executive director at Supergrid Europe.
The post Escaping the energy straitjacket appeared first on pv magazine Global.

asia-pacific.summary:
A Form Two male student died after collapsing during a rugby training session at a secondary school in Telok Mas, Malaysia’s Melaka state.
The 14-year-old is believed to have lost consciousness at about 6pm on Wednesday shortly after a break while listening to a briefing by his coach.
The victim’s granduncle, Yahya Endut, 64, said he rushed from Alor Gajah after receiving news of the incident involving the teenager, whom he had raised since childhood.

asia-pacific.title: Power of Hong Kong’s new national security regulation must be exercised prudently
asia-pacific.summary:
The recent enactment of the Safeguarding National Security (Procedural Matters) Regulation has generated significant interest and commentary on Hong Kong’s legal landscape.
It is recognised that maintaining national security is of paramount importance and that there is a constitutional responsibility on the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region to do so.
Under the Basic Law, that responsibility falls to a large extent on the city’s chief executive. It is also recognised in many common law...

asia-pacific.title: Hong Kong can take the regulatory high road amid US-China AI decoupling
asia-pacific.summary:
The market realignment over the past week starkly captures the self-defeating logic of America’s technology containment strategy.
The momentum began on June 15, when the shares of Chinese artificial intelligence pioneer Zhipu AI, which trade in Hong Kong under Knowledge Atlas Technology, surged by 48 per cent intraday after it announced the open-source release of its GLM-5.2 foundation model. The rally continued this week, with Zhipu’s shares trading at an intraday high of HK$2,980 (US$380) in..

asia-pacific.title: Chinese boy destroys US$30,000 worth of phones in home fire; dad’s response shocks everyone
asia-pacific.summary:
A boy in southern China accidentally ignited dozens of mobile phones while playing at home, but his father’s remarkably calm reaction has garnered praise online.
The father, surnamed Peng, operates a mobile phone shop in Shenzhen and utilises part of his home as a workshop for storing devices, according to the mainland media outlet First Scene.
The fire began when Peng’s five-year-old son noticed sparks from a short-circuiting power strip and, intrigued, used them to ignite a tissue.

asia-pacific.title: Hong Kong Science Fair marks five years of youth innovation
asia-pacific.summary:
[The content of this article has been produced by our advertising partner.]
For five years in a row the Hong Kong Science Fair has worked to build up the talent pipeline that will support scientific discovery and innovation in Hong Kong and across the country. 
By sparking ideas in children and instilling an entrepreneurial spirit in their formative years, it helps turn young curiosity into real-world solutions that support scientific discovery and innovation. 
The government is fully committed.

asia-pacific.title: Hong Kong prime office vacancy hits 7-month low on Central spillover demand
asia-pacific.summary:
Hong Kong’s overall prime office vacancy rate fell to a seven-month low in May, driven by spillover demand from Central, where premium offices were close to full occupancy, according to JLL.
With Central’s grade A office vacancy rate holding steady at 9.2 per cent, tenants appeared to have found their way to neighbouring Wan Chai and Causeway Bay. Vacancy there edged down to 9.8 per cent in May from 10.4 per cent in April, the lowest in 10 months, the property consultancy said in its latest...

asia-pacific.title: Powerful earthquakes rock Venezuela, bringing down buildings in Caracas
asia-pacific.summary:
⁠Strong earthquakes struck west of Venezuela’s capital ⁠on Wednesday afternoon, bringing down buildings in Caracas and causing scientists to estimate “high casualties and extensive damage” to the South American country.
A magnitude 7.2 earthquake struck about 160km (100 miles) west of Caracas, with a magnitude 7.5 tremor hitting less than a minute later, ‌according to the US Geological Survey (USGS).
“High casualties and extensive damage are probable and the disaster is likely widespread,” the..

asia-pacific.title: General who was last US soldier out of Afghanistan suddenly steps down
asia-pacific.summary:
The US Army’s commander of its forces in Europe and Africa – who was famously the last American soldier to leave Afghanistan in 2021 – is unexpectedly stepping down from his post after just 18 months in the job, the Army confirmed late on Tuesday.
General Christopher Donahue, commanding general of US Army Europe and Africa and commander of Nato’s Allied Land Command, will relinquish his command on July 2, according to an Army statement.
